From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

21st United States national census

| Twenty-first census of the United States | |
| --- | --- |
| ---   ← [1980](/wiki/1980_United_States_census "1980 United States census")  **April 1, 1990**  [2000](/wiki/2000_United_States_census "2000 United States census") →   --- | |
| U.S. Census Bureau seal | |
|  | |
| General information | |
| Country | United States |
| Results | |
| Total population | 248,709,873 (Increase 9.8%) |
| Most populous state | [California](/wiki/California "California") 29,760,021 |
| Least populous state | [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") 453,588 |

The **1990 United States census**, conducted by the [Census Bureau](/wiki/United_States_Census_Bureau "United States Census Bureau"), determined the resident population of the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") to be 248,709,873, an increase of 9.8 percent over the 226,545,805 persons [enumerated](/wiki/Enumeration "Enumeration") during the [1980 census](/wiki/1980_United_States_census "1980 United States census").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

Approximately 16 percent of households received a "long form" of the 1990 census, which contained more than 100 questions. Full documentation on the 1990 census, including census forms and a procedural history, is available from the [Integrated Public Use Microdata Series](/wiki/Integrated_Public_Use_Microdata_Series "Integrated Public Use Microdata Series").

It was the first census to designate "[Native Hawaiian and Other Pacific Islander](/wiki/Pacific_Islander_Americans "Pacific Islander Americans")" as a racial group separate from [Asians](/wiki/Asian_Americans "Asian Americans"). The census was also the first census to be directed by a woman, [Barbara Everitt Bryant](/wiki/Barbara_Everitt_Bryant "Barbara Everitt Bryant").

To increase black participation in the 1990 United States census, the bureau recruited [Bill Cosby](/wiki/Bill_Cosby "Bill Cosby"), [Magic Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson"), [Alfre Woodard](/wiki/Alfre_Woodard "Alfre Woodard"), and Miss America [Debbye Turner](/wiki/Debbye_Turner "Debbye Turner") as spokespeople.[[2]](#cite_note-2) [Aggregate data](/wiki/Aggregate_data "Aggregate data") for small areas, together with electronic boundary files, can be downloaded from the [National Historical Geographic Information System](/wiki/National_Historical_Geographic_Information_System "National Historical Geographic Information System"). [Personally identifiable information](/wiki/Personally_identifiable_information "Personally identifiable information") will be available in 2062.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

This was the first census since 1880 in which [Chicago](/wiki/Chicago "Chicago") was not the second-largest city, having been overtaken by [Los Angeles](/wiki/Los_Angeles "Los Angeles"). As of the [2020 census](/wiki/2020_United_States_census "2020 United States census"), Los Angeles has remained the nation's second-largest city. This was also the first census in which [Wyoming](/wiki/Wyoming "Wyoming") was the least populous state.

## Reapportionment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1990_United_States_census&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Reapportionment")]

The results of the 1990 census determined the number of seats that each state receives in the [United States House of Representatives](/wiki/United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States House of Representatives") starting with the [1992 elections](/wiki/United_States_elections,_1992#United_States_House_of_Representatives "United States elections, 1992"). Consequently, this affected the number of votes each state has in the [Electoral College](/wiki/Electoral_College_(United_States) "Electoral College (United States)") for the [1992 presidential election](/wiki/1992_United_States_presidential_election "1992 United States presidential election").

Because of population changes, twenty-one states had changes in their number of seats. Eight states gained at least one seat, and thirteen states lost at least one seat. The final result involved 19 seats being switched.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

| Gained seven seats | Gained four seats | Gained three seats | Gained one seat | Lost one seat | Lost two seats | Lost three seats |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| [California](/wiki/California%27s_congressional_delegations "California's congressional delegations") | [Florida](/wiki/United_States_congressional_delegations_from_Florida "United States congressional delegations from Florida") | [Texas](/wiki/Texas%27s_congressional_delegations "Texas's congressional delegations") | [Arizona](/wiki/Arizona%27s_congressional_delegations "Arizona's
